qa_pairs.question: How can users access most academic software that is freely available on the internet?
qa_pairs.answer: Users can email Alliance staff at support@tech.alliancecan.ca with a URL and ask them to install the package, making it available via a `module load` command for all users.

qa_pairs.question: What conditions must be met for Alliance staff to install academic software?
qa_pairs.answer: The license terms and technical requirements of the software must be met for Alliance staff to make it available.

qa_pairs.question: Are users permitted to install software in their own home space or project space?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, users are permitted to install software in their own home space or project space if they wish.

qa_pairs.question: What are some reasons a user might choose to install software in their own space?
qa_pairs.answer: A user might choose to install software in their own space if they plan to make their own modifications to the code, or if they wish to evaluate it quickly.

qa_pairs.question: What is a very common instruction pattern for installing software?
qa_pairs.answer: A very common instruction pattern is `configure; make; make install`.

qa_pairs.question: What are some variations of the `configure; make; make install` instruction pattern?
qa_pairs.answer: Variations include `cmake .` replacing `./configure`, and `sudo make install` replacing `make install`.

qa_pairs.question: Why might `make install` sometimes fail?
qa_pairs.answer: `make install` might sometimes fail because the package expects to be able to write to `/usr/local` or some other shared area in the file system.

qa_pairs.question: Why will `sudo make install` always fail on the system?
qa_pairs.answer: `sudo make install` will always fail because `sudo` is a request for "root" or administrator privileges, which users typically do not have.

qa_pairs.question: What is the usual solution when `make install` fails due to permission issues?
qa_pairs.answer: The usual solution is to supply a `--prefix` flag at the `configure` step, to direct the installation to go to a directory of your choice.

qa_pairs.question: Can you provide an example command using the `--prefix` flag for software installation?
qa_pairs.answer: An example command is `./configure --prefix=/my/project/directory/some-package && make && make install`.

qa_pairs.question: Whom should users contact if other errors arise during software installation?
qa_pairs.answer: If other errors arise during software installation, users should contact support at support@computecanada.ca.

qa_pairs.question: What is the simplest way to make use of a library on an Alliance system?
qa_pairs.answer: Normally, the simplest way to make use of a library on an Alliance system is to first load the corresponding module.

qa_pairs.question: What is the command to load a library module?
qa_pairs.answer: The command to load a library module is `module load library_name/x.y.z`.

qa_pairs.question: How do you include a library in the link phase of your build process after loading its module?
qa_pairs.answer: With the module loaded, you can modify the link phase of your build process to include the library, for example, `gcc -o my_prog file1.o file2.o -lnetcdf` if linking with the NetCDF library.

qa_pairs.question: What prefix is needed on the link line for a library name?
qa_pairs.answer: The link line needs to contain `-l` prefixed to the library name.

qa_pairs.question: What file extensions do library files typically have?
qa_pairs.answer: Library files typically have the extension `.a` or `.so`.

qa_pairs.question: When should a library module be loaded?
qa_pairs.answer: You will need to load the library module when you wish to run the software, not only during the building of it.

qa_pairs.question: What environment variables are set when a library module is loaded, and what do they point to?
qa_pairs.answer: Loading a library module will set environment variables `CPATH` and `LIBRARY_PATH` pointing to the location of the library itself and its header files.

qa_pairs.question: How do compilers use `CPATH` and `LIBRARY_PATH`?
qa_pairs.answer: Most compilers, such as Intel and GCC, automatically try the directories listed in `CPATH` and `LIBRARY_PATH` during compilation and linking phases.

qa_pairs.question: Does using `CPATH` and `LIBRARY_PATH` eliminate the need to specify library locations explicitly with `-I` and `-L`?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, this feature allows you to easily link against the library without specifying its location explicitly by passing the `-I` and `-L` options to the compiler.

qa_pairs.question: In what situations might it be necessary to explicitly specify the location of a library, even after loading its module?
qa_pairs.answer: In some cases, particularly with `cmake`, it may be necessary to specify explicitly the location of the library provided by the module.

qa_pairs.question: What is the preferred way to explicitly specify a library's location, especially with `cmake`?
qa_pairs.answer: The preferred and most robust way is to use an EasyBuild environment variable, `EBROOT...`, instead of manually typing a path.

qa_pairs.question: What is an example of an `EBROOT...` environment variable used with `cmake` for the GSL library?
qa_pairs.answer: For example, if you need to specify the location of the GSL library, the option you provide to `cmake` might look like `-DGSL_DIR=$EBROOTGSL`.

qa_pairs.question: How are `EBROOT...` environment variables constructed?
qa_pairs.answer: The `EBROOT...` environment variables adhere to the construction pattern of `EBROOT` followed by the name of the package, for example, `EBROOTGCC`.

qa_pairs.question: If software instructions include `apt-get` or `yum` commands, what should a user do?
qa_pairs.answer: If software instructions include `apt-get` or `yum` commands, it is unlikely that the user will be able to install it using those instructions; they should look for 'build from source' instructions or contact support for assistance.
